conjecture: The Eisenstein lattice snap is a contraction mapping: for any two points p,q, the distance between snap(p) and snap(q) is at most the distance between p and q
decomposition_raw:
[{"id": "S1", "statement": "For N random points p in the Euclidean plane, snap(p) is the nearest Eisenstein integer lattice point to p", "verifier": "hex_closest_pack", "args": {"N": "positive integer"}, "reason": "Confirms that the snap function correctly computes the closest Eisenstein lattice point to any input point, which is the definition of the snap operation here."}, {"id": "S2", "statement": "For N random points p in the Euclidean plane, snap(snap(p)) = snap(p)", "verifier": "snap_idempotence", "args": {"N": "positive integer"}, "reason": "Confirms that lattice points are fixed points of the snap function, as every output of snap is a lattice point."}, {"id": "S3", "statement": "For N random points p in the Euclidean plane, the Euclidean distance between p and snap(p) is at most 1/√3", "verifier": "covering_radius", "args": {"N": "positive integer"}, "reason": "Verifies the maximum snapping error for the Eisenstein lattice, a fundamental property of its Voronoi cells."}]

The full conjecture that the Eisenstein lattice snap is a contraction mapping cannot be verified using only the provided local verifiers, as it requires a general proof about the nonexpansiveness of nearest-point projections onto convex sets, which cannot be confirmed by the available random sampling tests.
